title: 2D Arrays
description: Arrays of arrays — declaring a grid, accessing with [row][col], and looping with nested loops.
objectives: Access and modify elements using row and column indices
Loop over a 2D array with nested for loops

sections.text: A 1D array is a row of values. A 2D array is a grid — rows and columns — which Java implements as an array where each element is itself another array.
An array whose elements are themselves arrays, commonly visualized as a grid of rows and columns and accessed with two indices: array[row][col].

sections.title: Spreadsheet analogy
sections.text: Think of a 2D array like a spreadsheet: the first index picks the row, the second picks the column within that row. grid[1][2] means "row 1, column 2" — the value 6 in the example above.
2. Declaring a 2D Array with a Fixed Size

sections.code: int[][] grid = new int[3][4]; // 3 rows, 4 columns, all default to 0

sections.text: grid[1][2] means row index 1, column index 2 — both zero-based, same as 1D arrays. It's easy to accidentally swap row and column in your head; always read it as [row][col].

grid.length gives the number of ROWS (3 here). grid[row].length gives the number of COLUMNS in that specific row (also 3 here, but rows could theoretically have different lengths — see the next section). The inner println() with no arguments moves to a new line after each row finishes printing.
5. Rows Don't Have to Be the Same Length

Because a 2D array in Java is really an array of separate array objects, each inner array can have its own independent length. This is called a jagged array. This is why the loop in the previous section always used grid[row].length for the inner bound, rather than assuming every row is the same size.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Swapping row and column in your head — grid[row][col], always in that order.
Assuming every row has the same length and using a single .length for both loop bounds instead of grid[row].length for the inner loop.
Forgetting a 2D array is really an array of arrays — grid.length is the ROW count, not the total element count.
Off-by-one errors in the nested loop bounds, same risk as with 1D arrays but doubled across two dimensions.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a 2D array in Java, conceptually?
questions.answer: An array whose elements are themselves arrays — commonly visualized as a grid of rows and columns, accessed with two indices as array[row][col].

questions.question: What does grid.length give you for a 2D array? What about grid[0].length?
questions.answer: grid.length gives the number of rows (the outer array's size). grid[0].length gives the number of columns in row 0 specifically — the size of that particular inner array.

questions.question: What is a jagged array?
questions.answer: A 2D array where the inner arrays (rows) have different lengths from each other, which is possible because each row is genuinely a separate array object in Java.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A 2D array is Java's array-of-arrays, typically visualized as a grid and accessed with two indices, array[row][col], both zero-based. grid.length gives the number of rows; grid[row].length gives the number of columns in that specific row, which matters because Java allows jagged arrays where rows have different lengths. Looping over a 2D array uses nested for loops, with the inner loop's bound checked per-row (grid[row].length) rather than assuming a uniform row size.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: 2D array = a spreadsheet. First bracket picks the ROW, second bracket picks the COLUMN. Rows can even be different lengths — it's really just an array of independent arrays.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: In grid[row][col], which index is the row and which is the column?
What does grid.length return? What about grid[0].length?
